characters.blurb: King of Sparta whose death left a pregnant queen. Lycurgus ruled as guardian until Charilaus was born.
Son of Polydectes. Lycurgus placed the child on the throne and later fled suspicion—then returned with the great reforms.
Named among Lycurgus’s ancestors in the Eurypontid line; part of the tangled royal pedigree Plutarch rehearses.
Early king who pressed Helots and Arcadians; cited to show how martial the house was before Lycurgus’s laws.
Link in the Eurypontid king-list Plutarch gives when tracing Lycurgus’s blood.
Elian who, with Lycurgus, is said to have ordered the Olympic games and the sacred armistice.
Later Spartan king who instituted (or strengthened) the ephorate—the check on kings that tempered Lycurgus’s design.

characters.relation: King who tried to restore the laws
characters.blurb: Reformer-king who sought to revive Lycurgus’s equality; Plutarch’s fuller tale of him is another Life.

characters.relation: Youth who struck out his eye
characters.blurb: Angry young Spartan who blinded Lycurgus in one eye during a riot over the messes; Lycurgus made him a household pupil instead of punishing him.
Named with Theopompus in traditions about amending the Great Rhetra after the people grew bold in assembly.
The Delphic “constitution” Lycurgus brought home: tribes, elders, assembly—and later a clause curbing popular amendments.
Through Delphi Lycurgus sought divine warrant for his laws; the rhetra is cast as Apollo’s voice.
One of the two places named in the rhetra between which the Spartan assembly was to meet.
Stream or bridge paired with Babyca as the bounds of the Lycurgan assembly ground.
Subject population bound to the land. Lycurgus’s system rested on their labor—and on the crypteia’s terror.
Lesbian lyre-poet said to have settled civic strife at Sparta with music—an art Lycurgus’s city still prized.
Elegist whose marching songs stiffened Spartan courage; cited among the cultural props of the Lycurgan city.
Musician-sage Plutarch links to the civilizing arts Sparta admitted even while distrusting fancy rhetoric.
Fifth-century general Plutarch cites as proof that Lycurgus’s breed of austere courage still appeared in later wars.
Often invoked when Plutarch weighs Sparta’s mixed constitution against ideal schemes.

characters.relation: Critic of parts of the system
characters.blurb: Noted flaws in Spartan women, property, and ephors even while granting Lycurgus’s stature as a lawgiver.
Both Spartan royal houses claimed Heraclid blood—the pedigree that framed Lycurgus’s right to reshape the state.
Author whose anecdotes about Lycurgus’s travels and death Plutarch sometimes follows.
Cited for the story that Lycurgus starved himself abroad so Sparta would not alter his laws.
Appears among later Spartans whose careers show the city’s long martial afterlife under Lycurgan habits.

Spartan reformer who—whether by oracle, travel, or force of character—gave his city its lasting constitution of equality, common meals, and trained courage. He starved himself abroad at the last so Sparta could never undo its oath to keep his laws.
The three hundred at Thermopylae are Plutarch's proof that Lycurgus's training produced men who could face death as a duty; Leonidas is the supreme example of the laws' endurance.

characters.relation: Great Spartan king; the laws' finest product
characters.blurb: Cited as the living proof that Lycurgus's discipline still produced great commanders centuries later—and that Sparta's decline set in when wealth broke the customs.

characters.relation: Ancestor; founder of the royal line
characters.blurb: Early king of Sparta from whom the Eurypontid house took its name—the dynastic line Plutarch traces when establishing Lycurgus's right to reform the state.

characters.relation: Navarch who bent the laws with wealth
characters.blurb: Brilliant admiral whose victory at Aegospotami brought silver and gold flooding into Sparta—the wealth Lycurgus's iron currency was meant to exclude, and the beginning of decline.
Plutarch says Lycurgus found Homer's works in Ionia—or had them from Creophylus's descendants in Samos—and was the first to bring them to Greece in ordered form.
Noted that Sparta, stripped of its monuments, would seem small to later ages—while its true strength, the disciplined city Lycurgus built, would astonish them.
Called Sparta a city of divine men who held elder-counsel, young-courage, and choruses of the Muses—a phrase Plutarch cites to show what the whole Greek world saw in the Lycurgan order.

characters.relation: Patron goddess of Sparta's Bronze House
characters.blurb: Athena Poliachos was worshipped at the famous Bronze House on the Spartan acropolis—the civic goddess whose temple framed Lycurgus's founding dedications.
Sicilian historian cited when Plutarch tries to fix the generation in which Lycurgus lived—a question without a secure answer even in antiquity.

characters.relation: Ancient Spartan cited on divorce law
characters.blurb: A very old Spartan Plutarch quotes on the customs concerning divorce and women—his answers, laconic to the point of comedy, illustrate the Lycurgan spirit perfectly.

characters.relation: Spartan elder; example of Laconic wit
characters.blurb: Cited for one of the memorable one-line Spartan answers Plutarch collects—the kind of compressed wisdom Lycurgus's culture prized above all rhetorical flourish.

characters.relation: Musical theorist who noted Sparta's songs
characters.blurb: Peripatetic writer on music cited by Plutarch as evidence that Sparta cultivated disciplined song and lyre—the arts Lycurgus admitted because they served courage, not pleasure.
Asked an old Spartan what above all preserved Sparta; the answer—contempt for wealth—sums up the Lycurgan principle Plutarch repeats throughout the Life.
Later counted among the Seven Wise Men of Greece; associated with the ephoralty—the office Theopompus added as a check on kings—and with the terse wisdom Lycurgus's city bred.

characters.relation: Poet who named Lycurgus's father as Prytanis
characters.blurb: The poet Simonides asserted that Lycurgus was the son of Prytanis—alone against all other genealogists who made Eunomus the father. Plutarch cites him as the singular dissenting voice.

characters.relation: Chronologer who placed Lycurgus before the Olympics
characters.blurb: Alexandrian polymath who, with Apollodorus, computed from king-list successions that Lycurgus lived much earlier than the Olympic games—contradicting Aristotle's claim that Lycurgus helped found them.
Chronological writer who joined Eratosthenes in arguing from Spartan king-lists that Lycurgus lived before the Olympic games—the scholarly counter-position to Aristotle.
Athenian historian and soldier; Plutarch cites a passage of his placing Lycurgus as contemporary with the first Heraclid kings—making him far earlier than later Spartan tradition allowed.

characters.relation: Poet in whose family Homer's works were preserved
characters.blurb: Samian epic poet said to have been Homer's companion or son-in-law; it was in the hands of his descendants in Samos that Lycurgus first encountered Homer's poems and resolved to bring them to Sparta.

characters.relation: Brother of Polydectes's queen; suspicious rival
characters.blurb: Brother of the queen mother who warned Lycurgus bluntly that he would soon be king—suggesting the infant Charilaus might not survive—and so poisoned the atmosphere that forced Lycurgus into voluntary exile.

characters.relation: Athenian nursed by a Spartan woman; contrast case
characters.blurb: Athenian statesman famously nursed by a Spartan woman and yet ruined by poor Athenian upbringing thereafter—Plutarch's vivid contrast with Lycurgus's state-controlled paideia that left nothing to a parent's whim.

characters.relation: Alcibiades's guardian; chose a bad tutor
characters.blurb: Athenian statesman who, as guardian of Alcibiades, chose the slave Zopyrus as his ward's tutor—Plato's example and Plutarch's foil for the Lycurgan system that put all boys in the state's hands.

characters.relation: Slave preceptor chosen by Pericles for Alcibiades
characters.blurb: A common slave selected by Pericles to supervise Alcibiades's education—no better than any household servant, Plato notes—and so the emblem of Athenian negligence toward the young versus Lycurgus's rigorous public training.

characters.relation: Athenian writer who reported on Spartan cups
characters.blurb: Athenian oligarch and writer; Plutarch cites his report that Spartan cups were lacquered dark to hide the murky look of river water—a detail showing Sparta made even utensils serve military practicality.

characters.relation: Author of a treatise on Sparta's constitution
characters.blurb: Wrote a work on the Lacedaemonian constitution; he disputes the tradition that Lycurgus lost an eye, arguing the blow merely wounded him without destroying his sight—and that Lycurgus built his temple to Athena Optiletis in gratitude for a cure.

characters.relation: Sophist who called Lycurgus a great soldier
characters.blurb: Hippias of Elis, polymath sophist who catalogued Olympic victors; he asserted that Lycurgus was himself a skilled and experienced military commander—a view Plutarch weighs against evidence for peaceful lawgiving.

characters.relation: Source attributing cavalry reform to Lycurgus
characters.blurb: Hellenistic writer who credited Lycurgus with the first division of cavalry into companies of fifty drawn up in a square—though Demetrius of Phalerum denied it, insisting Lycurgus legislated entirely in peacetime.

characters.relation: Peripatetic statesman who denied Lycurgus was a soldier
characters.blurb: Demetrius of Phalerum, Athenian statesman and Peripatetic writer, argued the opposite of Hippias: that Lycurgus made all his laws in a time of unbroken peace—consistent with the Olympic truce he helped establish.

characters.relation: Sophist who sat silent at the Spartan common table
characters.blurb: Invited to the Spartan public mess, this sophist said not a word throughout dinner; when others reproached him for silence, Archidamidas defended him: 'He who knows how to speak knows also when.'

characters.relation: Spartan king famous for laconic answers
characters.blurb: Eurypontid king cited for a characteristic Spartan retort: when pressed to name the best man in Lacedaemon, he finally said, 'He, Sir, that is the least like you.'

Spartan king and son of the controversial regent Pausanias; he delivered the laconic rejoinder that the Lacedaemonians alone of all the Greeks had 'learned none of your bad qualities' when an Athenian said they had no learning.
Spartan Agiad regent whose career ended in controversy; mentioned in the Life only as the father of Plistoanax—a line that situates the laconic anecdote in the royal house.

characters.relation: Citharist who joked about Spartan jurisdiction
characters.blurb: Athenian musician and wit; Plutarch cites his jest that Athenians should conduct religious processions, Eleans should preside at Olympia, and if either did amiss the Lacedaemonians should be beaten—a comic tribute to Sparta's role as Greece's taskmaster.

characters.relation: Socratic philosopher; quip about Theban pride
characters.blurb: Pupil of Socrates who quipped, when the Thebans were elated after Leuctra, that they looked like schoolboys who had beaten their master—a serious compliment to Sparta as Greece's pedagogical superior.

characters.relation: Philosopher who took Lycurgus as a model
characters.blurb: Cited alongside Plato and Zeno as one of the great political philosophers who took Lycurgus for their model—though they left only words, while Lycurgus built an actual state.

characters.relation: Stoic founder; took Lycurgus as model
characters.blurb: Founder of Stoicism, cited alongside Plato and Diogenes as a philosopher who held Lycurgus's polity as their ideal—yet produced only writings, not the realized commonwealth Lycurgus had actually made.

characters.relation: Tragedian buried at Arethusa; same end as Lycurgus
characters.blurb: Athenian tragic playwright who died and was buried at Arethusa in Macedonia; his tomb being struck by lightning—as Lycurgus's was—is cited as proof that divine marking by lightning is not unique to the lawgiver.

characters.relation: Source placing Lycurgus's death at Elis
characters.blurb: One of several authorities on the disputed location of Lycurgus's death; he says Lycurgus died after arriving at Elis—a tradition Plutarch records among several others (Cirrha and Crete).

characters.relation: Lycurgus's only son; died without issue
characters.blurb: The single son Lycurgus left behind; Antiorus died childless, extinguishing the lawgiver's direct line—though friends and relatives kept annual commemorations called Lycurgides for generations after.

characters.relation: Spartan navarch alongside Lysander and Agesilaus
characters.blurb: Spartan fleet-commander who served between the careers of Lysander and Agesilaus; Plutarch names him among the generals whom Greek cities in Asia welcomed as embodiments of Lycurgan discipline.
Archaic Spartan lyric poet whose choral songs the Helots were forbidden to sing—so completely had the masters identified his art with their own martial culture.

characters.relation: Father of Agis; king who presided over Sparta's decline
characters.blurb: Eurypontid king and father of Agis; Plutarch names him to mark the generation at which Lysander's flood of silver ended the five-century observance of Lycurgus's laws.

characters.relation: Spartan poet; Helots could not sing his verses
characters.blurb: Spartan lyric poet named alongside Terpander and Alcman as one whose songs the Helots were forbidden by their masters to perform—a measure of how completely music was woven into Spartan identity.

nameResolutions.note: Queen's brother who suspected Lycurgus of plotting against Charilaus—not King Leonidas
King Leonidas of Thermopylae; his wife is cited for Spartan female courage
King Leonidas making a characteristic laconic remark
